back_translation: After this, the Moabites and Ammonites, with some of the Meunites, came to make war against Jehoshaphat.
Men came and told Jehoshaphat, “A great army is coming against you from Edom, from beyond the Sea; they are now at Hazazon-tamar” (that is, En-gedi).
Jehoshaphat was afraid and set his heart to seek the LORD, and he proclaimed a fast throughout Judah.
Judah gathered to seek help from the LORD; they came from all the cities of Judah to seek the LORD.
Jehoshaphat stood in the assembly of Judah and Jerusalem, in the house of the LORD before the new courtyard,
and said, “O LORD, God of our fathers, are You not God in heaven? Do You not rule over all the kingdoms of the nations? Power and might are in Your hand, and no one can stand against You.
O our God, did You not drive out the inhabitants of this land before Your people Israel and give it forever to the descendants of Abraham Your friend?
They have lived in this land and built a sanctuary for Your Name here, saying,

‘If disaster comes upon us—sword, judgment, plague, or famine—we will stand before this house and before You, for Your Name is in this house, and cry out to You in our distress, and You will hear and save.’
Now, behold, the Ammonites, Moabites, and the men of Mount Seir, whom You did not allow Israel to invade when they came out of Egypt, so Israel turned away from them and did not destroy them—
behold, how they repay us, coming to drive us out of the possession You gave us as an inheritance.
O our God, will You not judge them? For we have no power against this great army that comes against us. We do not know what to do, but our eyes are on You.”
All Judah stood before the LORD, with their little ones, their wives, and their children.
Then the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jahaziel son of Zechariah, son of Benaiah, son of Jeiel, son of Mattaniah, a Levite of the sons of Asaph, in the midst of the assembly.
He said, “All Judah and Jerusalem, and King Jehoshaphat, listen! Thus says the LORD: ‘Do not be afraid or discouraged because of this great army, for the battle is not yours, but God's.’
Tomorrow go down against them; behold, they will come up by the Ascent of Ziz, and you will find them at the end of the valley before the Wilderness of Jeruel.

You will not need to fight in this battle. Take your positions, stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD on your behalf, O Judah and Jerusalem. Do not be afraid or discouraged; tomorrow go out against them, for the LORD is with you.’”
Jehoshaphat bowed down to the ground, and all Judah and Jerusalem fell down before the LORD, worshiping Him.
The Levites of the Kohathites and Korahites stood up to praise the LORD, the God of Israel, with a very loud voice.

They rose early and went out to the Wilderness of Tekoa; as they set out, Jehoshaphat stood and said, “Hear me, Judah and Jerusalem: believe in the LORD your God, and you will be established; believe His prophets, and you will prosper.”

He consulted with the people and appointed singers to the LORD, praising the splendor of His holiness; as they went out before the army they sang, “Give thanks to the LORD, for His steadfast love endures forever.”
When they began to sing and praise, the LORD set ambushes against the Ammonites, Moabites, and men of Mount Seir who had come against Judah, and they were defeated.
The Ammonites and Moabites rose against the inhabitants of Mount Seir, destroying them utterly; and when they had finished off the inhabitants of Seir, they turned to help destroy one another.
When Judah came to the watchtower overlooking the wilderness, they looked toward the great army, and behold, there were only dead bodies fallen on the ground; none had escaped.

Jehoshaphat and his people came to take the spoil, and found among the bodies abundant goods, garments, and precious things, more than they could carry; they spent three days gathering the spoil, it was so much.
On the fourth day they assembled in the Valley of Beracah, for there they praised the LORD; therefore that place is called the Valley of Beracah to this day.
Then all the men of Judah and Jerusalem, with Jehoshaphat at their head, returned to Jerusalem with joy, for the LORD had made them rejoice over their enemies.
They came to Jerusalem with harps, lyres, and trumpets, into the house of the LORD.
The fear of God came upon all the kingdoms of the lands when they heard that the LORD had fought against the enemies of Israel.
So the kingdom of Jehoshaphat was at peace, for his God gave him rest on every side.
Jehoshaphat reigned over Judah. He was thirty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned twenty-five years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Azubah daughter of Shilhi.
He walked in the way of Asa his father and did not turn from it, doing what was right in the eyes of the LORD.
But the high places were not removed, and the people had not yet set their hearts on the God of their fathers.
The rest of the acts of Jehoshaphat, from first to last, are written in the chronicles of Jehu son of Hanani, which are included in the Book of the Kings of Israel.
After this, Jehoshaphat king of Judah allied himself with Ahaziah king of Israel, who acted wickedly.
He joined him in building ships to go to Tarshish; they built the ships in Ezion-geber.

Then Eliezer son of Dodavahu of Mareshah prophesied against Jehoshaphat, saying, “Because you have allied with Ahaziah, the LORD has destroyed your works.” And the ships were wrecked and could not go to Tarshish.
